task: Write a Python function merge_within(pairs) taking a list of [start, end] inclusive integer ranges, possibly unsorted, and returning the merged ranges sorted by start, as a list of [start, end] lists. Ranges that overlap or share an endpoint merge: [1, 4] and [3, 6] become [1, 6], and [1, 3] and [3, 5] become [1, 5]. Ranges separated by any gap stay separate. An empty list returns []. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: The function correctly merges overlapping ranges, but could be improved for conciseness and readability
trace: I approached this task by first sorting the input list based on the start points of the ranges. Then, I iterated through the sorted list and compared each range with the last merged range. If the new range starts after the end point of the last merged range, it was appended to the merged_list. Otherwise, if the new range overlaps with the last merged range or shares an endpoint, I updated the end point of the latest merged range. This approach ensures that the output is sorted by start and handles the edge case where the starting point of a new range is less than the end point of the last merged range.
